content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Plus ANN search, Google TPUs, and GPUs for deep learning, decoded
Six new blogs this week, covering the frameworks that build LLM apps, the engines that serve them fast, the search that powers them, and the chips underneath it all.
Let’s get started.

How does LangChain work?
LangChain is a framework that helps us build applications powered by Large Language Models.
The core idea of LangChain is very simple: take small building blocks and chain them together to build a complete flow. Each block does one small job, and the output of one block becomes the input of the next block. This is the chain.
LangChain gives us ready-made building blocks. We just connect these blocks like Lego pieces.
Read here: https://outcomeschool.com/blog/how-does-langchain-work

How does LangGraph work?
LangGraph is a framework that helps us build applications powered by an LLM, where the work is organized as a graph of steps.
In simple words, LangGraph lets us describe an AI application as a set of steps, and it controls which step runs, in what order, and what happens next.
We describe our AI application as a graph of nodes and edges, we let a shared state carry the data, we use conditional edges to make decisions, and we use a checkpointer for memory and human approval. And one key point to always remember: the LLM only recommends which tool to use, and the agent actually calls the tool.
Read here: https://outcomeschool.com/blog/how-does-langgraph-work

How does SGLang work?
SGLang is a high-performance serving framework for large language models and multimodal models. In simple words, it is a tool that takes a large language model and serves its answers to many users at the same time, as fast as possible.
SGLang was built around one central idea. If two requests share the same starting text, they can share the saved work for that text. The technique that makes this sharing possible is called RadixAttention, and it is the heart of SGLang.
The shared prefix is processed once, and that saved work is reused by other requests that share it.
Read here: https://outcomeschool.com/blog/how-does-sglang-work

How does Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search work?
Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search means finding a neighbor that is very close to the true nearest one, but much faster, by allowing a tiny chance of not being perfectly exact.
Instead of checking every item, we use clever shortcuts that look at only a small part of the collection and still find an answer that is almost always correct. ANN organizes the data in advance so that, at search time, it can jump to the right neighborhood and ignore everything else.
So, ANN trades a tiny bit of accuracy for a massive gain in speed. For almost every real app, this trade is more than worth it.
Read here: https://outcomeschool.com/blog/how-does-approximate-nearest-neighbor-ann-search-work

How does a Google TPU work?
A TPU is a special computer chip made by Google. Its full name is Tensor Processing Unit. In simple words, a TPU is a chip built for one main job: doing the math that machine learning needs, and doing it very fast.
The heart of the design is the Systolic Array, a large grid of tiny calculators arranged in rows and columns. In a normal chip, the result is sent back to memory again and again, which is slow. In a Systolic Array, the calculators pass their results directly to their neighbors. The data does not keep running back to memory. It just keeps moving forward through the grid.
This is the secret. The TPU keeps the data moving and the calculators busy, instead of wasting time talking to memory.
Read here: https://outcomeschool.com/blog/how-does-a-google-tpu-work

How does a GPU work for Deep Learning?
A GPU is a special chip inside a computer that is very good at doing a huge number of simple calculations at the same time.
Here is the most important idea: deep learning is mostly matrix multiplication, and matrix multiplication is millions of small independent sums done at the same time. A CPU has a few very powerful cores that work one after another. A GPU has thousands of smaller, simpler cores that all work together.
For one hard problem, the single professor wins. But for a million simple sums, the thousands of students finish much faster, because they all work together. That is exactly why the GPU became the heart of modern AI.
Read here: https://outcomeschool.com/blog/how-does-a-gpu-work-for-deep-learning

That’s it for now.
